text.title: Service Blueprint Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Service Blueprint application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The blueprint MUST contain exactly five swimlanes in order: Customer Actions, Frontstage Interactions, Line of Visibility (divider), Backstage Actions, Support Processes & Systems. Missing or merged lanes are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without the Line of Visibility, the artefact regresses to a journey map; it is the entire point of the format.

rule.statement: The Support Processes & Systems lane MUST name specific internal systems (Salesforce, Zendesk, Stripe Billing, Snowflake) per journey step, never generic labels.
rule.rationale: Generic labels do not produce a backlog; named systems route work to the right team.

rule.statement: Every observed friction point MUST trace a downward arrow through the Line of Visibility to a specific back-stage step or system; un-traced friction is rejected.
rule.rationale: The arrow IS the fix-target — it converts UX frustration into ops/engineering work.

rule.statement: The blueprint MUST tag >=3 moments-of-truth with impact score (1-5), evidence source (interview / NPS / ticket / churn), and hypothesised root cause.
rule.rationale: Flat blueprints imply all steps matter equally; scoring forces prioritisation, the only path to a real backlog.

rule.statement: Every back-stage action MUST name the role/team that performs it (Tier-1 Support, Onboarding Specialist, SRE). 'The team', 'we', 'engineering' are rejected.
rule.rationale: Once the role is named, RACI inputs follow directly and you can ask whether that role has the tools/SLA to do the work.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
